_meta.description: Latin America subset of the retirement city dataset, structured to match the master schema. All scores are 1-10 (10 = best). Monthly costs in USD for a single person, comfortable lifestyle.
_meta.modeled_on: Lisbon (id 2 in master dataset): a safe, coastal, developed city with strong healthcare, good internet, an established expat community, and retiree-friendly visa access. Medellín and Mexico City are the closest Latin America analogues for top-tier retirement value.

schema_notes.scores: All *_score fields are 1-10 integers. 10 = best/most favorable for retirees.
schema_notes.monthly_cost: USD, single person, comfortable lifestyle (not budget, not luxury).
schema_notes.visa_ease_score: 10 = easy long-stay visa or retirement visa available. 1 = very restrictive. Colombia (Pensionado), Mexico (Residente Temporal/Permanente), Panama (Jubilado), Costa Rica (Pensionado), Ecuador (Jubilado), and Uruguay (Rentista) all offer relatively accessible retirement visa pathways.
schema_notes.healthcare_score: 10 = world-class private hospitals, low cost. 1 = poor infrastructure.
schema_notes.safety_score: 10 = very safe. 1 = high crime or instability.
schema_notes.climate_score: 10 = year-round pleasant. Subjective; filter by climate_type.
schema_notes.overall_retirement_score: Weighted composite. Recalculate when user adjusts priority weights.
schema_notes.ids: Integer ids here are local to this Latin America file (1-100). The master table uses UUID primary keys, so renumber or drop on import as needed.

_meta.supabase_table_sql: CREATE TABLE retirement_cities (id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), city TEXT NOT NULL, country TEXT NOT NULL, region TEXT NOT NULL, subregion TEXT, latitude FLOAT, longitude FLOAT, monthly_cost_usd INTEGER, lifestyle_label TEXT, description TEXT, tags TEXT[], visa_ease_score INTEGER, healthcare_score INTEGER, safety_score INTEGER, climate_score INTEGER, expat_community_score INTEGER, internet_score INTEGER, overall_retirement_score FLOAT, climate_type TEXT, language TEXT, currency TEXT, time_zone TEXT, popular_neighborhoods TEXT[], visa_notes TEXT, healthcare_notes TEXT, best_for TEXT[], avoid_if TEXT[], expense_shares JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW());

cities.description: The 'City of Eternal Spring' — once infamous, now one of Latin America's most celebrated expat destinations, with a perfect climate, modern metro, and low cost of living.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia's Pensionado visa requires proof of a pension/income of ~$750/month. Renewable annually; leads to permanent residency after 5 years. Very retiree-friendly.
cities.healthcare_notes: Excellent private hospitals (Clínica Las Américas, Hospital Pablo Tobón Uribe). Private health insurance is affordable (~$80-150/month). Quality rivals the US at a fraction of the cost.
best_for: Those wanting a large expat community

cities.description: Colombia's high-altitude capital — a sprawling cosmopolitan city with world-class restaurants, museums, and a thriving arts scene at 2,600m elevation.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Bogotá offers the best infrastructure for visa processing in the country.
cities.healthcare_notes: Home to Colombia's top hospitals (Fundación Santa Fe, Clínica del Country). Medical tourism destination. Excellent specialist availability.
best_for: Those wanting a big-city lifestyle at low cost

cities.description: Colombia's jewel on the Caribbean — a walled colonial city with colorful streets, white beaches, and a vibrant social scene.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Cartagena is popular with retirees seeking Caribbean coastal life.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica Blas de Lezo and Hospital Universitario del Caribe cover most needs; complex cases may go to Bogotá or Medellín.
avoid_if: Those intolerant of tropical heat and humidity

cities.description: Colombia's oldest European-founded city — a Caribbean gateway flanked by Sierra Nevada mountains and Tayrona National Park.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Smaller infrastructure than Cartagena; some services require trips to Barranquilla.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Central Julio Méndez Barreneche and private clinics available; serious cases go to Barranquilla or Bogotá.
best_for: Those wanting a smaller coastal town
avoid_if: Those wanting a large expat community

cities.description: The salsa capital of the world — a warm, energetic city in the Cauca Valley with a young population and a lively cultural scene.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Cali is less popular with expats than Medellín but offers lower costs.
cities.healthcare_notes: Fundación Valle del Lili is a leading regional hospital; private clinics widely available.

cities.description: Colombia's fourth-largest city and Caribbean gateway — known for its iconic carnival and growing commercial hub status.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Good air connectivity makes it practical for medical travel or family visits.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica General del Norte and Hospital Universidad del Norte provide solid coverage.
best_for: Those wanting Caribbean access without tourist prices
avoid_if: Those sensitive to intense heat and humidity
Anyone wanting an established expat scene

cities.description: A hillside coffee-region city with a spring-like climate similar to Medellín, far fewer tourists, and some of the cheapest costs in Colombia.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Low income requirement is easy to meet for most retirees.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Universitario de Caldas and Clínica Versalles serve the area well for a mid-sized city.
best_for: Those wanting a quieter alternative to Medellín
avoid_if: Those wanting a large English-speaking community

cities.description: The 'Pearl of the Coffee Region' — a warm, affordable city at the heart of Colombia's UNESCO-listed coffee landscape.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Pereira's airport has direct flights to major Colombian cities.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica Los Rosales and Hospital Universitario San Jorge provide solid regional coverage.
best_for: Budget retirees wanting a warm climate
avoid_if: Anyone wanting a large expat community
Those needing highly specialized medical care

cities.description: The smallest of Colombia's coffee-region cities — slow-paced, inexpensive, and surrounded by coffee farms and nature reserves.
cities.visa_notes: Colombia Pensionado visa applies. Smallest city in the trio; some services require travel to Pereira or Manizales.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital del Quindío and local private clinics available; specialist cases often refer to Pereira.

cities.description: One of the world's great megacities — a cultural powerhouse with world-class museums, dining, and neighborhoods at a fraction of US costs.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico's Residente Temporal visa requires proof of ~$1,600/month income or sufficient savings. Renewable for up to 4 years; then eligible for permanent residency.
cities.healthcare_notes: Top private hospitals (Hospital Ángeles, Hospital ABC) offer US-quality care at 30-50% of US prices. Extensive specialist availability.
avoid_if: Anyone wanting a quiet small-town feel

cities.description: Mexico's second city and cultural heartland — birthplace of mariachi and tequila, with a pleasant highland climate and a massive expat community in the Lake Chapala area.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Lake Chapala and Ajijic nearby host the largest English-speaking expat community in Latin America.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Country 2000, Puerta de Hierro, and Hospital San Javier offer excellent private care at low cost.
best_for: Those wanting a large English-speaking community
avoid_if: Minimalists wanting a quiet escape from expat culture
Anyone on an ultra-tight budget (costs higher than smaller cities)

cities.description: A colonial gem famed for its cuisine, indigenous art, and mezcal — a slower-paced cultural haven beloved by artists and food-focused retirees.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Oaxaca's growing expat community makes local support easier than a decade ago.
cities.healthcare_notes: Private hospitals like Star Médica and Hospital Reforma handle most needs; complex cases go to Mexico City.
best_for: Those wanting a colonial small-city feel
avoid_if: Those needing advanced specialist care nearby

cities.description: The 'White City' of the Yucatán — a beautiful colonial capital with a strong expat scene, Mayan heritage, and Caribbean beach access within an hour.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Mérida is frequently ranked among Mexico's safest large cities.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Star Médica Mérida and Clínica de Mérida provide solid private care; complex cases may go to Cancún or Mexico City.
best_for: Those wanting beach access without living on the coast
avoid_if: Those sensitive to humidity and heat (May-September is brutal)

cities.description: Mexico's most celebrated expat town — a UNESCO World Heritage colonial city with a large North American retiree community, art galleries, and year-round festivals.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. San Miguel has one of the highest densities of North American retirees in Latin America — services cater accordingly.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital de la Fe and Hospital MAC provide private care; for serious cases, Querétaro or Mexico City (2.5 hrs) are options.
avoid_if: Budget retirees (premium prices for the expat market)
Those wanting authentic local immersion without tourism

cities.description: A beloved Pacific coast resort town with cobblestone streets, a vibrant art scene, and one of Mexico's most welcoming LGBTQ+ communities.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Strong expat support networks and bilingual services available.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital CMQ and San Javier Marina Hospital offer solid private care; complex cases go to Guadalajara.
best_for: Social retirees wanting a large expat community
avoid_if: Anyone wanting a quiet off-the-beaten-path experience

cities.description: A Pacific port city with a beautifully restored historic center, 21km of beaches, and a fraction of the crowds of other Mexican beach resorts.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Mazatlán is growing in expat popularity as a lower-cost alternative to Puerto Vallarta.
cities.healthcare_notes: Sharp Hospital and Clínica Cima Mazatlán provide reasonable private care; Guadalajara is a 4-hour option for specialists.
best_for: Those wanting a historic city plus coast
avoid_if: Anyone expecting the polish of Los Cabos or PV
Those needing advanced specialist hospitals locally

cities.description: The Riviera Maya's cosmopolitan hub — a walkable beach town with turquoise Caribbean water, cenotes, and a thriving international scene.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Strong international community with bilingual services throughout.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Galenia and Hospiten Playa del Carmen provide private care; serious cases go to Cancún.
best_for: Those wanting a walkable international town
avoid_if: Anyone wanting a tranquil, uncrowded environment

cities.description: The Riviera Maya's boho-chic destination — Mayan ruins above turquoise water, boutique eco-hotels, and a wellness-focused international community.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Tulum is experiencing rapid development and prices are rising quickly.
cities.healthcare_notes: Limited local medical infrastructure; residents rely on Playa del Carmen or Cancún for anything beyond basic care.
avoid_if: Budget retirees (high cost for the infrastructure)

cities.description: Baja California's glamorous tip — dramatic desert-meets-sea scenery, world-class sport fishing, golf, and a high-end resort lifestyle.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Many retirees enter and exit on tourist visas or maintain residency status.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital H+ Los Cabos and Blue Net Hospital provide private care; serious cases fly to Tijuana or the US.
best_for: Those wanting US conveniences with Mexican prices
avoid_if: Those wanting cultural immersion over resort living

cities.description: Mexico's industrial capital and wealthiest city — surrounded by dramatic Sierra Madre mountains, with excellent healthcare and proximity to the US border.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Proximity to Texas border (3 hrs) makes it practical for US healthcare access or family visits.
cities.healthcare_notes: One of Mexico's best healthcare hubs — Hospital Christus Muguerza and Centro Médico Zambrano Hellion offer world-class care.
best_for: Those wanting easy US border access
avoid_if: Those wanting a warm coastal lifestyle
Anyone concerned about US State Dept advisories for Nuevo León

cities.description: A UNESCO-listed colonial city gaining popularity as a quieter, safer, and slightly cheaper alternative to San Miguel de Allende.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. One of Mexico's safest and most livable cities according to Mexican government metrics.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Star Médica Querétaro and Hospital Angeles Querétaro offer excellent private care.
best_for: Those wanting colonial charm without San Miguel prices
Retirees wanting proximity to Mexico City
avoid_if: Anyone seeking a large established expat community

cities.description: Mexico's culinary capital — a stunning Baroque colonial city an hour from Mexico City, famous for mole, Talavera tiles, and stunning Popocatépetl views.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Puebla is an easy day trip from Mexico City and has a growing expat scene.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Star Médica Puebla and Hospital UPAEP provide quality private care.
best_for: Those wanting an authentic Mexican city without tourist saturation
avoid_if: Those wanting a large English-speaking expat community

cities.description: A kaleidoscopic colonial mining city tumbling down a ravine — one of Mexico's most visually dramatic and culturally rich small cities.
cities.visa_notes: Mexico Residente Temporal visa applies. Guanajuato is walkable but very hilly — mobility is a consideration for older retirees.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital General de Guanajuato and private clinics available; specialists typically in León (30 min away).
best_for: Those wanting a smaller colonial city with character
avoid_if: Anyone with limited mobility (extremely hilly, no cars in much of downtown)

cities.description: The 'Paris of South America' — a sophisticated metropolis with world-class dining, tango, incredible architecture, and a European cultural sensibility.
cities.visa_notes: Argentina's Rentista visa requires proof of ~$2,000/month income. The parallel exchange rate (blue dollar) significantly stretches USD income. Visa process can be bureaucratic.
cities.healthcare_notes: Excellent public hospital system and world-class private hospitals (Austral, Alemán, Italiano). Medical tourism destination, especially for dental work.
best_for: Those who benefit from the favorable USD exchange rate
avoid_if: Those concerned about economic instability and inflation
Anyone wanting a simple financial life

cities.description: Argentina's wine capital at the foot of the Andes — a leafy, sun-drenched city surrounded by world-famous Malbec vineyards and ski resorts.
cities.visa_notes: Argentina Rentista visa applies. USD stretches very far at parallel exchange rates. Mendoza has a small but established expat wine community.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Español and private clinics offer good care; Buenos Aires is a short flight for specialist needs.
best_for: Those wanting a quiet city with Andean scenery
avoid_if: Those concerned about Argentina's economic volatility

cities.description: Argentina's second city — a vibrant university hub with a rich Jesuit colonial heritage, active nightlife, and lower prices than Buenos Aires.
cities.visa_notes: Argentina Rentista visa applies. Córdoba is cheaper than Buenos Aires while offering most of the same urban amenities.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Privado and Clínica Vélez Sarsfield provide solid care; strong medical school presence.
best_for: Those wanting a young, energetic city atmosphere
avoid_if: Those wanting a large expat community
Anyone seeking a quiet, slow-paced retirement

cities.description: A sprawling Paraná River port city and birthplace of Che Guevara — known for its beaches, nightlife, and strong local identity.
cities.visa_notes: Argentina Rentista visa applies. Safety concerns have increased in Rosario in recent years.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Provincial del Centenario and several private clinics available.
best_for: Those wanting authentic Argentine city life
avoid_if: Those wanting an established expat scene

cities.description: Argentina's favorite Atlantic beach resort — a large coastal city with casino culture, fresh seafood, and beautiful dunes.
cities.visa_notes: Argentina Rentista visa applies. Mar del Plata is mainly popular with Argentine retirees rather than international expats.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Privado de la Comunidad and several regional hospitals serve the large population well.
best_for: Those immersing in local Argentine culture
avoid_if: International expats wanting an English-speaking community

cities.description: South America's alpine jewel — a Patagonian lake-and-mountain resort town with Swiss-influenced architecture, skiing, and breathtaking scenery.
cities.visa_notes: Argentina Rentista visa applies. Bariloche is fairly isolated; Buenos Aires is a 2-hour flight.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Regional Bariloche covers basic and moderate needs; serious cases are air-transferred.

cities.description: Northwest Argentina's colonial gem — 'Salta la Linda' (Salta the Beautiful) with extraordinary Andean scenery, colonial architecture, and an authentic Argentine feel.
cities.visa_notes: Argentina Rentista visa applies. Salta is off the main expat radar — primarily Argentine retirees and adventure tourists.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital San Bernardo and private clinics serve the region; Buenos Aires is a 2-hour flight for complex care.
avoid_if: International expats wanting an English-speaking network

cities.description: Chile's modern, sophisticated capital — flanked by the Andes and 90 minutes from the Pacific, with excellent infrastructure, safety, and a stable economy.
cities.visa_notes: Chile's Rentista visa requires proof of sufficient income. Chile has one of Latin America's most stable visa systems. Process typically takes 2-4 months.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica Las Condes and Clínica Alemana are world-class. Chile has Latin America's best overall healthcare system.
best_for: Those wanting Latin America's safest major city
avoid_if: Budget retirees (similar cost to Southern Europe)

cities.description: Chile's bohemian port city — a UNESCO site of stacked houses, funicular lifts, and vibrant street art tumbling down 42 hills to the Pacific.
cities.visa_notes: Chile Rentista visa applies. Valparaíso is often combined with nearby Viña del Mar for a coastal lifestyle.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Carlos Van Buren and private clinics available; Santiago (1.5 hrs) is accessible for specialists.
avoid_if: Anyone with limited mobility (very steep hills)
Those wanting low crime rates (higher than Santiago)

cities.description: Chile's 'Garden City' — an upscale coastal resort city next to Valparaíso with manicured parks, beaches, a casino, and a calmer atmosphere.
cities.visa_notes: Chile Rentista visa applies. Viña del Mar is generally considered safer and more polished than neighboring Valparaíso.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica Reñaca and private clinics provide solid care; Santiago accessible for specialists.
best_for: Those wanting a quieter coastal city than Valparaíso
avoid_if: Anyone wanting bohemian or authentic gritty city feel

cities.description: Chile's 'City of Churches' — a colonial coastal city in Norte Chico, famous for its beaches, pisco production, and clear skies ideal for stargazing.
cities.visa_notes: Chile Rentista visa applies. La Serena is a major gateway to the Elqui Valley wine and pisco region.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Juan de Dios de La Serena and Clínica La Serena serve the region adequately.
avoid_if: Those wanting a large expat community

cities.description: Chile's adventure capital in the Lake District — a small town on a volcano-backed lake offering skiing, hiking, rafting, and geothermal hot springs.
cities.visa_notes: Chile Rentista visa applies. Pucón is a small town that swells significantly in summer; winters are quiet.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital San Francisco de Borja provides basic care; Temuco (1.5 hrs) for specialist needs.
avoid_if: Anyone wanting urban amenities or large expat community

cities.description: South America's culinary capital — a sprawling Pacific coast city with a world-famous food scene, striking colonial center, and excellent healthcare.
cities.visa_notes: Peru's Rentista visa requires proof of $1,000/month income. Straightforward application process. Leads to residency.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica Anglo Americana and Clínica Ricardo Palma provide excellent private care at low cost. Medical tourism destination.
best_for: Foodies (Lima ranks among the world's top food cities)
avoid_if: Sun-seekers (Lima is famously gray and overcast)

cities.description: The ancient Inca capital at 3,400m — a stunning highland city where Spanish colonial architecture overlays Inca stonework, gateway to Machu Picchu.
cities.visa_notes: Peru Rentista visa applies. Cusco attracts a significant expat community drawn to spiritual and indigenous culture.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica San Juan de Dios and Hospital Regional del Cusco available; Lima is a 1.5-hour flight for serious care.
avoid_if: Those sensitive to altitude (3,400m can be challenging)

cities.description: Peru's 'White City' built from white volcanic sillar stone — a charming highland city at 2,300m with a near-perfect climate and Colca Canyon access.
cities.visa_notes: Peru Rentista visa applies. Arequipa is Peru's second city and a growing alternative to Lima for expats.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica San Pablo and Hospital Goyeneche provide reasonable care; Lima is a 1.5-hour flight.
best_for: Adventure and nature lovers (Colca Canyon, volcanos)
avoid_if: Those wanting a large expat community

cities.description: Lima's upscale clifftop district overlooking the Pacific — the de-facto expat hub with the best restaurants, parks, and security in the city.
cities.visa_notes: Peru Rentista visa applies — same process as Lima. Miraflores is the premium district within Lima.
cities.healthcare_notes: Walking distance to Clínica Anglo Americana and Clínica Ricardo Palma — some of Peru's best private hospitals.
avoid_if: Those on a tight budget (most expensive Lima district)
Anyone wanting a quiet, small-town feel

cities.description: Peru's 'City of Eternal Spring' on the northern coast — a colonial city near Chan Chan (the world's largest adobe city) with a mild climate and very low costs.
cities.visa_notes: Peru Rentista visa applies. Trujillo is largely off the international expat radar but is popular with Peruvian retirees.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Regional de Trujillo and Clínica Peruano Americana available; Lima is a 9-hour bus or 1-hour flight.
best_for: History enthusiasts (Chan Chan, Huaca del Sol)

cities.description: The world's second-highest capital city (2,850m) — a stunning UNESCO colonial center, near-perfect climate, and one of the most accessible visa regimes in Latin America.
cities.visa_notes: Ecuador's Pensionado visa requires just $800/month pension income. Uses US dollars — no currency risk. One of Latin America's easiest retirement visa regimes.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Metropolitano and Hospital de los Valles offer solid private care. Medical tourism is growing here.
avoid_if: Anyone wanting a beach lifestyle (Quito is landlocked inland)

cities.description: Ecuador's most beloved expat city — a charming UNESCO colonial highland city with a spring climate, low costs, and one of the friendliest communities in Latin America.
cities.language: Spanish (English widely spoken in expat areas)
cities.visa_notes: Ecuador's Pensionado visa ($800/month) is among Latin America's easiest. USD economy, no currency exchange risk.
cities.healthcare_notes: Monte Sinaí and Clínica Santa Inés provide solid private care at very low cost. Dental work and routine care exceptionally affordable.
best_for: Those wanting a large, welcoming English-speaking expat community
avoid_if: Those wanting major city amenities and nightlife

cities.description: Ecuador's largest city and main port — a hot, humid river city with a revitalized waterfront and direct flights to the Galapagos.
cities.visa_notes: Ecuador Pensionado visa applies. Guayaquil is the main commercial hub but has seen increased security concerns in recent years.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Alcívar and Kennedy Hospital provide good private care for Ecuador.
best_for: Those wanting a major Ecuadorian city
avoid_if: Those sensitive to tropical heat and humidity

cities.description: Ecuador's legendary surf village — a laid-back Pacific beach town with a bohemian, youthful vibe and some of the best waves in South America.
cities.visa_notes: Ecuador Pensionado visa applies. Montañita is a very small town; most expats are surfers or hippie-lifestyle seekers.
cities.healthcare_notes: Very limited local healthcare — Santa Elena (30 min) or Guayaquil (2 hrs) for anything significant.

cities.description: Ecuador's 'Music Capital' — a small highland university city in southern Ecuador, known as one of the cheapest and most agreeable places to retire in Latin America.
cities.visa_notes: Ecuador Pensionado visa applies ($800/month minimum). Loja is very affordable even by Ecuador standards.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Isidro Ayora and private clinics serve the city; Cuenca is 3 hrs for more complex care.
best_for: Those wanting a quiet, safe highland town
avoid_if: Anyone wanting an established expat network

cities.description: The world's highest administrative capital at 3,640m — a surreal, dramatic Andean city with an Indigenous market culture and ultra-low living costs.
cities.visa_notes: Bolivia doesn't have a formal retirement visa but allows long-term residency through annual visas. Process can be complex.
cities.healthcare_notes: Limited private hospitals; Clínica Foianini in Santa Cruz is better. Altitude significantly worsens many health conditions.
best_for: Those fascinated by Andean Indigenous culture
avoid_if: Anyone with heart, lung, or circulation issues (extreme altitude)

cities.description: Bolivia's constitutional capital and UNESCO World Heritage city — a beautifully preserved whitewashed colonial city at a more manageable 2,750m.
cities.visa_notes: Bolivia annual visa approach applies. Sucre is Bolivia's most livable city for expats due to its pleasant climate and colonial character.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Santa Bárbara and private clinics available; serious cases go to Cochabamba or Santa Cruz.
best_for: Those wanting a pleasant highland climate
avoid_if: Those needing modern healthcare or specialist services
Anyone wanting a large expat community

cities.description: Bolivia's 'City of Eternal Spring' at 2,570m — a warmer, more livable highland city with a renowned food scene and the lowest cost of living in Bolivia.
cities.visa_notes: Bolivia annual visa approach applies. Cochabamba is Bolivia's agricultural center and has a somewhat larger middle class than La Paz.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica Los Olivos and Hospital Viedma provide regional coverage.
avoid_if: Those wanting expat infrastructure or English services

cities.description: Bolivia's fastest-growing and most modern city — a lowland tropical hub with better infrastructure than the highland cities and a more international feel.
cities.visa_notes: Bolivia annual visa applies. Santa Cruz is Bolivia's most accessible entry point by air and has the country's best private healthcare.
cities.healthcare_notes: Clínica Foianini and Hospital Los Olivos are Bolivia's best private hospitals.
best_for: Budget retirees wanting Bolivia's best infrastructure
Those in Bolivia for business reasons
avoid_if: Those wanting colonial character (Santa Cruz is modern and sprawling)

cities.description: South America's least-visited capital — a relaxed, affordable riverside city with very low taxes, easy residency, and a small but growing expat community.
cities.visa_notes: Paraguay's Rentista visa requires just $1,300/month income. Very favorable tax environment for foreign income (territorial tax system).
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Privado Francés and Clínica La Costa provide private care; serious cases often go to Buenos Aires.
best_for: Those wanting a very relaxed, low-key lifestyle
Budget retirees wanting a capital city
avoid_if: Those wanting a vibrant expat community or tourism infrastructure

cities.description: Consistently ranked one of Latin America's most livable cities — a relaxed, cultured capital with a strong social safety net, stable democracy, and beautiful coastline.
cities.visa_notes: Uruguay's Rentista visa requires proof of $1,500/month income. Process takes 6-12 months but leads to permanent residency and eventually citizenship.
cities.healthcare_notes: Uruguay's FONASA system and private mutualistas offer solid, comprehensive care. Asociación Española and Hospital Británico are reliable private options.
best_for: Those wanting Latin America's most stable and progressive country
avoid_if: Budget retirees (among Latin America's most expensive)
Those wanting a large English-speaking expat community

cities.description: South America's St. Tropez — a glamorous Atlantic resort peninsula where fashionable Argentines and Brazilians summer, with excellent beaches and a marina.
cities.visa_notes: Uruguay Rentista visa applies. Punta del Este is a seasonal resort — much quieter in winter (June-August).
cities.healthcare_notes: British Hospital Punta del Este and private clinics serve residents; Montevideo (1.5 hrs) for specialists.
best_for: Those wanting a prestigious coastal address
avoid_if: Those wanting year-round vibrant community (very quiet off-season)

cities.description: Uruguay's oldest city — a tiny, impossibly charming UNESCO colonial port town across the Río de la Plata from Buenos Aires.
cities.visa_notes: Uruguay Rentista visa applies. Colonia's proximity to Buenos Aires (1-hour ferry) makes it appealing as a base while keeping Argentine connections.
cities.healthcare_notes: Very limited local healthcare — Montevideo (2.5 hrs by car) or Buenos Aires (1 hr by ferry) for anything significant.
best_for: Those wanting extreme safety and tranquility
avoid_if: Anyone wanting a large community or urban amenities

cities.description: Central America's most cosmopolitan city — a modern skyscraper-studded capital with the legendary Canal, dollarized economy, excellent infrastructure, and one of Latin America's best retirement visa programs.
cities.visa_notes: Panama's Jubilado (Pensionado) visa is among the world's best retirement visas — requires just $1,000/month pension. Comes with extensive discounts on healthcare, entertainment, and restaurants.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Punta Pacífica (Johns Hopkins affiliate) and Hospital Nacional offer world-class care. Medical tourism hub for Central America.
best_for: Those wanting the best retirement visa in Latin America
Those wanting USD with no exchange risk
avoid_if: Tropical heat avoiders (hot and humid year-round)

cities.description: Panama's highland retiree paradise — a cool mountain town with world-famous coffee, flowers, and one of the most established North American expat communities in Central America.
cities.visa_notes: Panama Jubilado visa applies ($1,000/month pension). Boquete has extensive expat support services and English-language resources.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Chiriquí in nearby David (45 min) is a solid regional hospital. Routine care available in Boquete; Panama City for specialists.
best_for: Those wanting cool mountain climate in the tropics

cities.description: Panama's fishing village turned retiree gem — a tiny Pacific town on the Azuero Peninsula with excellent surf, whale watching, and a slow lifestyle.
cities.visa_notes: Panama Jubilado visa applies. Pedasi is tiny — most services require going to Santiago or Panama City.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic clinic only; Hospital Anita Moreno in Los Santos (1.5 hrs) for anything significant.
best_for: Those wanting a true small-town Pacific lifestyle
avoid_if: Those wanting urban amenities or large community

cities.description: A Caribbean archipelago of turquoise water, colourful stilted houses, and jungle — one of Panama's most beloved beach communities.
cities.visa_notes: Panama Jubilado visa applies. Island life means reliance on boat transport and limited infrastructure.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic hospital available; serious cases evacuate to David (1 hr flight) or Panama City.
avoid_if: Anyone wanting convenience and urban comforts

cities.description: Costa Rica's bustling capital — a gateway to the country's beaches, volcanoes, and rainforests, with good healthcare and a well-established expat infrastructure.
cities.visa_notes: Costa Rica's Pensionado visa requires $1,000/month pension. One of Latin America's most established retirement visa programs, in use for decades.
cities.healthcare_notes: CIMA Hospital and Clínica Bíblica offer world-class private care. Costa Rica's CAJA system covers residents for public healthcare.
best_for: Nature lovers using the capital as a base
Those wanting easy US flight connections
avoid_if: Those wanting a beach lifestyle (need to travel from San José)

cities.description: San José's upscale suburb — the preferred address for North American expats, with English-language services, US-style malls, and the best private healthcare in Costa Rica.
cities.visa_notes: Costa Rica Pensionado visa applies. Escazú has one of the densest North American expat populations in all of Central America.
cities.healthcare_notes: CIMA Hospital is a Joint Commission-certified hospital — Costa Rica's best private facility is minutes away.
best_for: North American retirees wanting maximum comfort and community
Those wanting English services without leaving
avoid_if: Those wanting authentic local culture immersion

cities.description: Costa Rica's most popular surf town — a lively Guanacaste beach community with excellent Pacific sunsets, a large expat population, and year-round sunshine.
cities.visa_notes: Costa Rica Pensionado visa applies. Liberia airport (45 min) has direct US flights.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic clinics in Tamarindo; Liberia Hospital (1 hr) for significant care; San José for specialists.
avoid_if: Anyone needing reliable advanced healthcare nearby

cities.description: Costa Rica's closest major beach to San José — a lively Pacific surf town just 90 minutes from the capital, popular with Tico and expat weekend crowds.
cities.visa_notes: Costa Rica Pensionado visa applies. Jacó's proximity to San José makes it easy to access specialist healthcare.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic clinics available; Hospital Monseñor Sanabria in Puntarenas (30 min) or San José (90 min).
best_for: Those wanting beach living with city access
avoid_if: Those wanting a tranquil, secluded retreat (Jacó can be busy and raucous)

cities.description: Costa Rica's most famous beach-and-rainforest destination — a lush Pacific community surrounding the national park, beloved for wildlife, sunsets, and natural beauty.
cities.visa_notes: Costa Rica Pensionado visa applies. Manuel Antonio is notably LGBTQ+ friendly.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic clinics in Quepos; Hospital Monseñor Sanabria (2 hrs) or San José for serious care.
best_for: Those wanting Costa Rica's iconic beach-jungle combination
avoid_if: Budget retirees (premium prices for the scenery)

cities.description: A wellness and surf mecca on the Nicoya Peninsula — one of the world's designated Blue Zones, famous for longevity and a health-conscious expat lifestyle.
cities.visa_notes: Costa Rica Pensionado visa applies. Nosara has strict development rules protecting the natural environment.
cities.healthcare_notes: Very limited local medical care; Nicoya (1.5 hrs) or San José for serious needs.
best_for: Those drawn to Blue Zone longevity culture
avoid_if: Budget retirees (one of CR's priciest small towns per amenity)

cities.description: The 'White City' of Guanacaste — Costa Rica's gateway to the Pacific beaches with a pleasant dry-season climate, lower costs, and a growing expat community.
cities.visa_notes: Costa Rica Pensionado visa applies. Direct international flights from US and Canada via Liberia International Airport.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Enrique Baltodano Briceño provides public care; private clinics available. Closer to beaches while maintaining hospital access.
best_for: Those wanting affordable Guanacaste base with beach access
Authentic Costa Rican city feel seekers
avoid_if: Those wanting to be on the beach
Rainy season visitors (extremely wet June-November in surrounding areas)

cities.description: Nicaragua's sprawling capital on Lake Managua — one of Central America's most affordable capitals, though the political situation has reduced expat confidence.
cities.visa_notes: Nicaragua's political environment post-2018 has created uncertainty for foreign residents. Visa stability is unpredictable.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Metropolitano Vivian Pellas is a solid private hospital; public system is underresourced.
best_for: Those with strong local ties or business
avoid_if: Anyone concerned about political stability and safety
Those wanting a stable retirement environment

cities.description: Nicaragua's jewel — a beautifully preserved colonial city on Lake Nicaragua that was once Central America's top expat retirement destination before political instability reduced its appeal.
cities.visa_notes: Nicaragua political risk applies. Granada had a thriving expat scene that has significantly contracted since 2018.
cities.healthcare_notes: Very limited private care; Managua (1 hr) for significant medical needs.
best_for: Those specifically interested in Nicaragua despite risks
Colonial architecture lovers on extreme budgets
avoid_if: Those wanting healthcare or infrastructure reliability

cities.description: Nicaragua's intellectual and revolutionary capital — a university city with stunning colonial architecture, active volcanoes, and extreme affordability.
cities.visa_notes: Nicaragua political risk applies. León has even fewer expat support services than Granada.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Escuela Óscar Danilo Rosales provides public care; very limited private options.
best_for: Those truly committed to Nicaragua on an ultra-tight budget
avoid_if: Anyone concerned about political situation or infrastructure

cities.description: Nicaragua's most popular beach town — a crescent-shaped Pacific bay with surf, sportfishing, and a laid-back backpacker-turned-expat scene.
cities.visa_notes: Nicaragua political risk applies. San Juan del Sur's expat community has reduced significantly post-2018.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic clinic only; Rivas (30 min) or Managua (2.5 hrs) for any real medical care.
best_for: Those set on Nicaragua despite risks

cities.description: Honduras's mountain capital — an affordable, undervisited city with a relatively mild highland climate, though crime rates remain a significant concern.
cities.visa_notes: Honduras offers a Pensionado/Jubilado visa for retirees. However, safety concerns significantly limit its appeal.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital CEMESA and Hospital La Católica offer private care; limited specialist availability.
best_for: Those with specific local ties or work in Honduras
avoid_if: Almost all independent retirees due to very high crime rates

cities.description: Honduras's industrial capital — a hot lowland city that serves as the commercial hub but has historically suffered from very high crime rates.
cities.visa_notes: Honduras Pensionado visa applies. San Pedro Sula is not recommended for retirees due to extreme safety concerns.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital del Valle and Hospital Médico Quirúrgico offer private care.
best_for: Business travelers transiting to other areas

cities.description: Honduras's Caribbean gem — a Bay Island with world-class diving, turquoise water, and an established English-speaking expat community that feels distinctly separate from mainland Honduras.
cities.visa_notes: Honduras Pensionado visa applies. Roatán feels entirely different from mainland Honduras — most expats live here specifically for the island lifestyle.
cities.healthcare_notes: Roatán International Hospital provides surprisingly good private care for an island; serious cases go to the US or mainland.
best_for: Those wanting a Caribbean island without the Caribbean prices

cities.description: The smallest and most affordable of the Bay Islands — a backpacker and diver haven famous for the cheapest PADI certification in the world.
cities.visa_notes: Honduras Pensionado visa applies. Utila is very small with very limited infrastructure.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic clinic only; emergency evacuation to La Ceiba or US required for serious cases.
best_for: Young-spirited retirees wanting backpacker island life

cities.description: Central America's largest city — a sprawling highland capital with excellent restaurants, shopping, and healthcare, though traffic and safety require navigating carefully.
cities.visa_notes: Guatemala's Rentista visa requires $1,500/month income. Process involves local attorney. 2-year renewable.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Herrera Llerandi and Centro Médico offer solid private care at low cost. Medical tourism growing.
best_for: Those using Guatemala City as a hub to access the country
avoid_if: Those wanting small-town charm (most prefer Antigua nearby)

cities.description: Guatemala's crown jewel and former colonial capital — a UNESCO World Heritage city of cobblestone streets, colorful facades, and stunning volcano backdrops.
cities.language: Spanish (English widely spoken in expat areas)
cities.visa_notes: Guatemala Rentista visa applies. Antigua has a large, active expat community and extensive English-language support services.
cities.healthcare_notes: Several private clinics available; Guatemala City (45 min) for hospital-level care.
avoid_if: Those wanting beach access (Antigua is inland)

cities.description: A tiny island town in Lake Petén Itzá — the gateway to Tikal, Guatemala's legendary Mayan ruins, surrounded by jungle lakes.
cities.visa_notes: Guatemala Rentista visa applies. Flores is extremely remote and has very limited infrastructure.
cities.healthcare_notes: Very limited; Guatemala City (8 hrs by road) or emergency air evacuation for serious care.
best_for: Mayan history enthusiasts making an extended stay
avoid_if: Anyone needing healthcare or urban amenities
Those wanting an established expat community

cities.description: El Salvador's capital, rapidly transforming under its Bitcoin-embracing government — a compact city with improving safety, volcanic scenery, and growing appeal for crypto-savvy expats.
cities.visa_notes: El Salvador's Rentista visa requires $1,000/month income; uses USD — no currency risk. Government has been very pro-foreigner investment.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital de Diagnóstico and Hospital Bautista provide private care.
avoid_if: Safety-cautious retirees (improving but still a concern)

cities.description: The Caribbean's largest city and oldest European-founded settlement in the Americas — a lively, tropical capital with a UNESCO colonial zone and strong infrastructure.
cities.visa_notes: Dominican Republic's Rentista visa requires $1,500/month income. Relatively straightforward process.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital General Plaza de la Salud and Clínica Abreu provide solid private care.
best_for: Those wanting a large Caribbean city
History buffs (first European settlement in Americas)
avoid_if: Those wanting beach living (need to travel outside the city)

cities.description: The Caribbean's resort capital — 32km of white-sand beaches on the eastern tip of Hispaniola, with a growing residential community beyond the all-inclusive hotels.
cities.language: Spanish (English widely spoken in resort areas)
cities.visa_notes: Dominican Republic Rentista visa applies. Punta Cana's infrastructure has improved significantly for permanent residents.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital Bávaro and several private clinics serve the area; Santo Domingo (3 hrs) for specialists.
best_for: Those wanting Caribbean without high cost of living
avoid_if: Those wanting authentic local culture (heavily touristic)

cities.description: The Dominican Republic's second city — a more authentic, less touristy alternative to Santo Domingo, at the heart of the Cibao valley's agriculture and cigars.
cities.visa_notes: Dominican Republic Rentista visa applies. Santiago is cheaper than Santo Domingo for the same quality of life.
cities.healthcare_notes: Hospital General Plaza de la Salud branch and private clinics available.
best_for: Those wanting an authentic Dominican city experience
avoid_if: Those wanting a large expat community

cities.description: The US commonwealth capital — a vibrant Caribbean city where colorful Old San Juan, tropical beaches, and US legal/financial systems converge.
cities.visa_notes: Puerto Rico is a US territory — US citizens can move freely with no visa required. Act 22/Act 60 offers major tax incentives for US citizens (0% capital gains tax on new gains).
cities.healthcare_notes: US Medicare accepted, US-standard hospitals and VA system. Hospital Auxilio Mutuo and Ashford Presbyterian are strong private options.
best_for: US citizens wanting Caribbean life under US law
Those wanting US systems with tropical flair
avoid_if: Those on a tight budget (comparable to US mainland costs)

cities.description: The frozen-in-time Caribbean capital — a hauntingly beautiful, crumbling Art Deco city of vintage cars, son cubano, and extraordinary culture, though severely limited infrastructure and politics.
cities.visa_notes: Cuba does not offer a standard retirement visa for most nationalities. Long-term stays require an artist visa, religious visa, or similar category. US citizens face significant legal and financial restrictions.
cities.healthcare_notes: Cuba has a system designed for Cubans, not foreigners paying in hard currency. Foreigners use Servimed clinics, which are better but expensive for Cuba.
best_for: Those with a deep fascination for Cuba's unique history and culture
avoid_if: Anyone needing reliable internet, utilities, or modern infrastructure

cities.description: Cuba's most perfectly preserved colonial town — a UNESCO World Heritage cobblestone gem frozen in the 18th century, with nightly salsa in the streets.
cities.visa_notes: Cuba visa restrictions apply. Trinidad is primarily a tourist destination; long-term foreign residence is extremely difficult.
cities.healthcare_notes: Very limited. The nearest significant medical facility is in Sancti Spíritus (1 hr).
best_for: Those on extended Cuban tourist visas who love colonial history
avoid_if: Anyone wanting reliable infrastructure, internet, or healthcare

cities.description: Cuba's premier beach resort peninsula — 20km of spectacular white sand and turquoise water in a resort-dominated enclave east of Havana.
cities.visa_notes: Cuba visa restrictions apply. Varadero is dominated by all-inclusive hotels; actual long-term residency options for foreigners are very limited.
cities.healthcare_notes: Basic clinic for tourists; Matanzas (1 hr) for hospital care.
best_for: Short-stay beach enthusiasts from Canada and Europe on extended tourist stays
avoid_if: Anyone wanting long-term residency or reliable infrastructure

cities.description: The Bahamas' pastel-hued capital — a Caribbean city of pirates' history, pink sand beaches, tax-free living, and the playground of the wealthy, with direct US flights.
cities.visa_notes: Bahamas Annual/Permanent Residency available. No income tax, no capital gains tax, no inheritance tax — major financial draw for high-net-worth individuals.
cities.healthcare_notes: Doctors Hospital Nassau is a solid private facility; serious cases fly to Miami (1 hr).
best_for: Those wanting Caribbean lifestyle with direct US access

cities.description: Grand Bahama's main city — smaller and more affordable than Nassau, with beautiful beaches and a more relaxed vibe, though still recovering from Hurricane Dorian (2019).
cities.visa_notes: Bahamas Residency options apply. Freeport is a free trade zone — additional tax advantages on the island itself.
cities.healthcare_notes: Rand Memorial Hospital provides public care; private clinics supplement. Serious cases to Nassau or Miami.
best_for: Those wanting Bahamas lifestyle at lower Nassau prices

cities.description: The Caribbean's most English of islands — Barbados's capital combines British colonial heritage, world-class cricket, rum, and stunning beaches with a stable, prosperous economy.
cities.visa_notes: Barbados Welcome Stamp (12-month remote work/retirement visa) and Special Entry Permit available. Attractive residency options for the right income levels.
cities.healthcare_notes: Queen Elizabeth Hospital (public) and Bayview Hospital (private) provide good care for the Caribbean.
avoid_if: Those wanting Latin culture (Barbados is very English Caribbean)

cities.description: The capital of one of the Eastern Caribbean's most beautiful islands — St. Lucia's dramatic Piton mountains, rainforests, and volcanic beaches make it a bucket-list retirement destination.
cities.visa_notes: St. Lucia offers a Retirement Programme and Residency by Investment. Relatively straightforward for those meeting income requirements.
cities.healthcare_notes: Victoria Hospital (public) and Tapion Hospital (private) are the main facilities; complex cases go to Barbados or Trinidad.

cities.description: The twin-island republic's buzzing capital — home to the world's greatest carnival, steel pan music, and the Caribbean's most cosmopolitan food scene, but with some crime concerns.
cities.visa_notes: Trinidad and Tobago has limited formal retirement visa options; most expats use extended residency permits. Oil-rich economy but bureaucracy can be complex.
cities.healthcare_notes: Port of Spain General Hospital (public) and Medical Associates Hospital (private) provide reasonable care.
best_for: Those wanting a non-touristy Caribbean capital
avoid_if: Those wanting beach lifestyle (Tobago island is better for that)

cities.description: Jamaica's gritty capital — a music-legend city of reggae, Blue Mountains coffee, and extraordinary cultural energy, though safety requires careful neighborhood selection.
cities.visa_notes: Jamaica's JOME (Jamaica One-Stop Mortgage/Residency) program and informal long-stay options exist. Process can be complex.
cities.healthcare_notes: University Hospital of the West Indies and Nuttall Memorial Hospital provide private care; advanced cases to the US.
best_for: Those wanting Caribbean on a budget
avoid_if: Those wanting resort-town living (Montego Bay better for that)

cities.description: Jamaica's second city and tourism capital — famous for Hip Strip, Doctor's Cave Beach, all-inclusive resorts, and the gateway to the island's best vacation areas.
cities.visa_notes: Jamaica residency options apply. Montego Bay has direct US/UK/Canadian flights and is Jamaica's most international city.
cities.healthcare_notes: Cornwall Regional Hospital (public) and Fairview Medical Centre (private) serve the area.
best_for: Those wanting Jamaica's most international atmosphere

cities.description: Jamaica's laid-back western tip — home to the famous Seven Mile Beach and a legendary sunset scene that anchors a bohemian, free-spirited expat lifestyle.
cities.visa_notes: Jamaica residency options apply. Negril is a small beach town — very limited services.
cities.healthcare_notes: Negril Health Centre; Savanna-La-Mar or Montego Bay for hospital care.
best_for: Those wanting a low-key Caribbean town
avoid_if: Anyone needing healthcare or amenity access
Those wanting a formal town structure

cities.description: Jamaica's garden parish on the north coast — lush tropical valleys, Dunn's River Falls, and a gentler pace than Montego Bay's bustle.
cities.visa_notes: Jamaica residency options apply. Ocho Rios is primarily a cruise port but has a residential community on outskirts.
cities.healthcare_notes: St. Ann's Bay Regional Hospital (30 min) for significant care; Kingston for specialists.
best_for: Those wanting a quieter North Coast alternative

cities.description: The Caribbean's offshore finance capital — a tax-free British territory with exceptional diving, Seven Mile Beach, and some of the world's cleanest, safest waters.
cities.visa_notes: Cayman Islands Residency by Investment requires significant financial investment (CI$1M+). UK passport holders have easier access. No income tax, capital gains tax, or inheritance tax.
cities.healthcare_notes: Cayman Islands Hospital and Health City Cayman Islands (world-class cardiac/orthopedic center) provide exceptional care for the Caribbean.
best_for: Wealthy retirees wanting Caribbean tax-free living with US-quality infrastructure
avoid_if: Budget retirees (one of the Caribbean's most expensive destinations)
Those wanting local culture over expat bubble

cities.description: The sailing capital of the Caribbean — the BVI's compact capital is the gateway to the world's finest cruising grounds, with crystalline waters and British orderliness.
cities.visa_notes: BVI Residency Certificate required for stays over 6 months. Obtaining permanent residency requires 20 years of residency — long-term is expensive and complex.
cities.healthcare_notes: Dr. D. Orlando Smith Hospital provides basic care; complex cases go to Puerto Rico or Florida.
best_for: Those wanting a British-administered tax-friendly territory

cities.description: St. Thomas's capital and the US Virgin Islands' commercial hub — a duty-free Caribbean paradise under US jurisdiction with American systems and spectacular harbor views.
cities.visa_notes: USVI is a US territory — US citizens can move freely without a visa. EDC Program offers major tax incentives (near-zero income tax) for qualifying individuals.
cities.healthcare_notes: Roy L. Schneider Hospital plus private clinics; complex cases fly to Puerto Rico or Florida.
best_for: US citizens wanting Caribbean life under US law with tax benefits

cities.description: The Dutch side's capital on this divided Caribbean island — a duty-free beach and casino town where planes famously buzz Maho Beach during landing.
cities.language: Dutch, English (English is the working language)
cities.visa_notes: Sint Maarten has its own residency rules as part of the Kingdom of the Netherlands. No income tax for residents. Dutch and EU citizens have advantages.
cities.healthcare_notes: St. Maarten Medical Center provides general care; complex cases go to Martinique or Puerto Rico.
avoid_if: Those wanting a quiet, natural island experience

cities.description: Aruba's colorful Dutch-Caribbean capital — on the 'Happy Island' outside the hurricane belt, with near-constant sunshine, white beaches, and a stable political environment.
cities.visa_notes: Aruba's Long Stay Permit (Permit voor Langer Verblijf) requires proof of income and health insurance. Part of the Kingdom of the Netherlands.
cities.healthcare_notes: Dr. Horacio E. Oduber Hospitaal is modern and well-equipped for an island; specialist cases to Colombia or the US.
best_for: Those wanting Caribbean with minimal hurricane risk
avoid_if: Those wanting lush tropical vegetation (very arid)

cities.description: Curaçao's UNESCO-listed capital — a kaleidoscopic Dutch-colonial port city of painted merchant houses reflected in the Sint Annabaai, with world-class diving.
cities.visa_notes: Curaçao Permit for Long Stay (similar to Aruba). Part of the Kingdom of the Netherlands. Relatively affordable option vs Aruba.
cities.healthcare_notes: Curaçao Medical Center (CMC) provides solid regional care; complex cases to Colombia or Netherlands.
avoid_if: Budget retirees (still expensive for the region)
Those wanting lush tropical scenery (very dry)

cities.description: South America's most culturally diverse capital — a UNESCO-listed Dutch colonial city where South Asian, African, Chinese, Amerindian, and European cultures coexist at the Amazon's edge.
cities.language: Dutch (Sranan Tongo and English also spoken)
cities.visa_notes: Suriname long-term residency is possible but process is not straightforward. Dutch speakers have advantages.
cities.healthcare_notes: Academic Hospital Paramaribo provides basic-to-moderate care; serious cases go to the Netherlands or Cayenne.
best_for: The truly adventurous, culturally curious retiree
avoid_if: Anyone needing reliable healthcare or internet

cities.description: The 'Garden City of the Caribbean' — a flat, colonial wooden-architecture capital on the Atlantic coast, gaining significance with Guyana's massive oil discoveries.
cities.visa_notes: Guyana has no formal retirement visa; long-term stays via work permits or residency applications. Oil industry growth is bringing more international infrastructure.
cities.healthcare_notes: Georgetown Public Hospital (public) and GPHC private wing; medical evacuation to Trinidad or the US for serious cases.
best_for: Those fascinated by Guyana's unique English-speaking South American culture
Oil industry workers transitioning to retirement
avoid_if: Those wanting established expat networks or advanced healthcare
